item.title: Harnessing Loss for Q-Modulated Biosensors
item.description:
Nature Photonics, Published online: 02 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41566-026-01942-y

A new nanophotonic sensing strategy converts even subtle refractive-index changes into large intensity variations via radiative Q-factor modulation. Implemented in a bound-state-in-the-continuum (BIC) metasurface, the concept enables ultrasensitive and scalable biosensing of only a few tens of tumour extracellular vesicles within 15 minutes.

item.title: Improved Perception for Machine Vision Under Complex Illumination
item.description:
Nature Sensors, Published online: 01 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s44460-026-00104-5

Optical interference from lasers and intense lighting can confuse machine vision. Now, a tunable band-stop photodetector has been designed that uses machine learning to suppress disruptive spectral components at the sensor, enabling more reliable perception in challenging illumination.

item.title: Tunable Band-Stop Photodetection with Machine Learning-Enabled Broadband Spectral Adaptation
item.description:
Nature Sensors, Published online: 01 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s44460-026-00098-0

A machine learning-guided tunable band-stop photodetector enables adaptive suppression of unpredictable light interference for robust broadband machine vision.

item.title: Competing Programs Shape Cortical Sensorimotor–association Axis Development
item.description:
Nature, Published online: 01 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10699-x

Multispecies evidence supports the multinodal induction–exclusion in network development model, in which sensorimotor-to-association patterning is governed by competing processes of induction and exclusion driven by two opposing transcriptomically defined programs.

item.title: Nanodiamonds Made from Tiny Graphene Triangles
item.description:
Nature, Published online: 01 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02001-w

Nanodiamonds can host atom-sized light emitters for quantum sensing and imaging, but making nanodiamonds that are small, crystalline and uniform has been difficult. A single-step process for making nanodiamonds only 3–4 nanometres in size uses planar carbon ‘nanographene’ molecules with hydrogen atoms on the edges, and can be adapted to generate fluorescent nanodiamonds.

item.title: AI Clones in Conversation at Academic Conferences
item.description:
Nature Sensors, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44460-026-00089-1

Artificial intelligence (AI) clones could serve as a new interface for scientific communication between publishers and research communities. At a Nature conference in Seoul, multimodal AI clones — including one of the Chief Editor of Nature Sensors — engaged researchers in real-time dialogue about publishing and careers, in three different languages.

item.title: Non-Contact Biomechanical Profiling of 3D Tissues
item.description:
Nature Sensors, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44460-026-00096-2

A wireless biomechanical sensing platform enables non-contact, scalable, cost-effective profiling of three-dimensional (3D) cardiac organoids and engineered heart tissues.

item.title: Wireless and Contactless Biomechanic Well Plate for Monitoring Cardiac Organoid and 3D-Tissue Contraction
item.description:
Nature Sensors, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44460-026-00087-3

A wireless and contactless biomechanical well plate leverages ultrasensitive silicon cantilevers to enable high-throughput, real-time quantification of contractile forces in cardiac organoids and engineered heart tissues.

item.title: Transparent and Flexible MXene Antennas Take Shape
item.description:
Nature Reviews Materials, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41578-026-00940-4

An article in Science Advances reports a MXene grid architecture that delivers a conformal, transparent and efficient antenna platform suitable for both wearable devices and flexible metasurfaces.

item.title: A Global Map of Seagrass Ecosystems
item.description:
Nature, Published online: 24 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01805-0

Combining satellite imagery and machine learning has created the first comprehensive map of seagrass meadows, in a boost for the conservation of these crucial ecosystems.

item.title: Chiral Laser Gyroscopes Breaking the Lock-In Limit
item.description:
Nature, Published online: 24 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10684-4

Ring laser gyroscope lock-in has been eliminated using spontaneous symmetry breaking in a He–Ne laser, enabling accurate near-zero rotation sensing without external components, improving miniaturization and precision.

item.title: How AI Is Revealing the Secret Lives of Animals from Hummingbirds to Pumas
item.description:
Nature, Published online: 15 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01887-w

Advances in machine learning and other technologies are helping researchers to trace the movements, landmarks and social practices of wildlife.

item.title: Integrated Thermal Sensing of Vascular Morphology for Haemodynamic Monitoring
item.description:
Nature Sensors, Published online: 12 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44460-026-00082-8

A wearable thermal sensing approach enables non-invasive inference of vascular depth and diameter from skin thermal transients, improving the accuracy of haemodynamic modelling.

item.title: In-Sensor Spectral Decoding for Efficient Spectroscopic Recognition
item.description:
Nature Sensors, Published online: 12 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44460-026-00085-5

In-sensor spectral decoding enables direct spectral reconstruction and application-level recognition without off-chip computation, delivering efficient spectroscopic sensing.

item.title: 3D Printing Methods Unite to Engineer Capillary Networks
item.description:
Nature Chemical Engineering, Published online: 10 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44286-026-00401-3

Organ-scale bioprinting requires the incorporation of biomimetic vascular networks for nutrient delivery and waste removal to maintain cell viability and function. Now, a machine learning-assisted hybrid bioprinting technique enables the formation of hierarchical channels within 3D printed constructs that can be endothelialized to produce functional vasculature.

item.title: Go with the Flow
item.description:
Nature Chemical Engineering, Published online: 10 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44286-026-00397-w

A ring-shaped electrochemical ion-pumping architecture synchronizes fluid flow with circuit switching. The result is pseudo-continuous, unidirectional ion transport, which enables redox-free, energy-efficient and scalable desalination.

item.title: Multiparametric Molecular Imaging of the Brain
item.description:
Nature Sensors, Published online: 09 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44460-026-00088-2

Multiparametric molecular imaging of the brain

item.title: Real-Time On-Device Dynamic Quantification of Sensor Output Uncertainty
item.description:
Nature Sensors, Published online: 09 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44460-026-00086-4

Real-time on-device dynamic quantification of sensor output uncertainty

item.title: Multi-Mode Optoelectronic Devices Enable Compact and Versatile In-Sensor Computing
item.description:
Nature Sensors, Published online: 05 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44460-026-00083-7

Achieving dynamic reconfiguration between linear sensing, optoelectronic synaptic plasticity, and direct light-induced neuronal spiking within a single two-terminal retinomorphic sensor provides a scalable path toward high-density versatile in-sensor computing.

item.title: Nanofluidic Ionic Memory for Next-Generation Computing
item.description:
Nature Reviews Materials, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41578-026-00919-1

In the brain, memory involves release of neurotransmitters and transport of ions through nanoconfined channels. This Perspective discusses how nanofluidic memristors emulate this confined ion transport, highlighting the materials, design strategies and challenges involved in developing brain-inspired computing technologies.
